items.title: Using the diameter instead of the radius (or vice versa) in area calculations
items.description: Students substitute the diameter directly where the radius is required (or apply A = πd²/4 but then separately also halve it), producing an area that is off by a factor of 4 from the correct value and propagating a 4x error into stress and deformation answers.
items.whyItHappens: The area formula A = πd²/4 already accounts for the radius internally (since r = d/2 means πr² = πd²/4); students sometimes additionally try to convert d to r before using this form, double-applying the conversion.
items.correction: Use A = πd²/4 directly with the diameter, OR A = πr² directly with the radius — never mix the two forms in the same calculation.

items.title: Forgetting that the angle of twist formula gives radians, not degrees
items.description: Students compute phi = TL/(JG) and report the raw numerical result as degrees, when the formula (with T, L, J, G in consistent SI units) gives the answer in radians. This produces an answer that looks numerically small and is roughly 57 times too small if reported as degrees directly.
items.whyItHappens: Most other angle quantities encountered in earlier coursework (geometry, surveying) are conventionally reported in degrees, so the radian result feels unfinished and gets reported as-is.
items.correction: Multiply the radian result by 180/π (≈57.2958) to convert to degrees before reporting, unless the problem explicitly asks for radians.

items.title: Swapping b and h in the rectangular moment of inertia formula
items.description: For I = bh³/12, students sometimes cube the width b instead of the depth h (i.e., compute hb³/12 when bending is about the horizontal centroidal axis), giving a moment of inertia that is incorrect by a factor of (h/b)² or (b/h)² and a correspondingly wrong bending stress.
items.whyItHappens: The formula's b and h are easy to transpose because both are just 'the two cross-section dimensions' without an obvious visual cue once the numbers are substituted; the convention is that h (cubed) is always the dimension parallel to the direction of bending.
items.correction: Before substituting, explicitly identify which dimension is parallel to the bending direction (that one is h, and gets cubed) and which is perpendicular (that one is b).
